sections.plain_summary: Microscopic colitis is a type of inflammatory bowel disease that causes chronic, watery, non-bloody diarrhoea. Unlike Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis, the colon looks normal to the naked eye during colonoscopy — the inflammation is only visible under a microscope, which is why a biopsy is essential for diagnosis. It is most common in middle-aged and older women and is strongly linked to certain medications, particularly NSAIDs and proton pump inhibitors.
sections.detailed_summary:
Microscopic colitis (MC) is a chronic inflammatory condition of the large intestine characterised by colonic mucosal inflammation that is invisible on standard colonoscopy but detectable on tissue biopsy. There are two main subtypes: collagenous colitis (CC), which involves a thickened subepithelial collagen band, and lymphocytic colitis (LC), which involves an increased density of intraepithelial lymphocytes. A third pattern, incomplete microscopic colitis, is also recognised.

The hallmark symptom is chronic, watery, non-bloody diarrhoea — often voluminous and urgent, sometimes occurring five to ten or more times per day. Nocturnal diarrhoea, which wakes people from sleep, is a particularly suggestive feature that helps distinguish MC from functional conditions like IBS. Abdominal cramping, bloating, faecal incontinence, and fatigue are common. Weight loss may occur in more severe cases.

The exact cause is not fully understood, but MC is thought to involve an abnormal immune response to luminal antigens — including gut bacteria, bile acids, and dietary components — in genetically susceptible individuals. A striking feature of MC is its strong association with specific medications: NSAIDs (especially aspirin and ibuprofen), proton pump inhibitors (PPIs), SSRIs, and statins are the most commonly implicated. Removing the offending drug often leads to significant improvement or full remission, making medication review a critical first step.

Autoimmune conditions — coeliac disease, thyroid disorders, rheumatoid arthritis, type 1 diabetes — co-occur with MC at higher-than-expected rates, suggesting shared immune dysregulation. Smoking is an independent risk factor and is associated with more severe disease. Bile acid malabsorption may coexist and contribute to diarrhoea in a subset of people.

Diagnosis requires colonoscopy with multiple biopsies from different colonic segments, including the right colon and transverse colon, where changes may be patchy. Many people with MC have had years of symptoms and been told they have IBS before a biopsy-confirmed diagnosis is made. Treatment options include budesonide (an oral corticosteroid with localised action), bismuth subsalicylate, cholestyramine, and — when medications are the trigger — drug withdrawal. A significant proportion of people achieve remission with budesonide, though relapse on cessation is common and maintenance therapy may be needed.

prevalence.summary: More common than most people realise — particularly in older women — and frequently misdiagnosed as IBS for years.
prevalence.estimate: Approximately 10–20 cases per 100,000 person-years in Western populations; estimated lifetime prevalence around 0.2–0.5%
prevalence.notes: Prevalence estimates vary considerably by region and are likely underestimates because MC requires biopsy for diagnosis and many patients are never scoped. Incidence has increased markedly over recent decades, possibly reflecting greater diagnostic awareness and rising NSAID/PPI use. Women are affected 3–5 times more often than men for collagenous colitis; lymphocytic colitis shows a smaller sex difference. Peak incidence is in the 60s and 70s, though MC can occur at any age.

subgroups.name: Collagenous colitis (CC)
subgroups.description: Characterised by a thickened subepithelial collagen band (≥10 µm) on biopsy. More strongly female-predominant and more often associated with medications such as NSAIDs and PPIs.
distinguishing_features: Thickened collagen band visible on haematoxylin and eosin stain
May present with more severe diarrhoea
Higher association with NSAID and PPI use
More commonly associated with coeliac disease

subgroups.name: Lymphocytic colitis (LC)
subgroups.description: Characterised by increased intraepithelial lymphocytes (≥20 per 100 epithelial cells) without collagen thickening. More equal sex distribution than CC.
distinguishing_features: Elevated intraepithelial lymphocyte count on biopsy
Strong association with coeliac disease and gluten
May respond to gluten withdrawal in those with coeliac disease or NCGS
Associated with SSRI and NSAID use

subgroups.name: Incomplete microscopic colitis
subgroups.description: Histological changes that do not fully meet the threshold for CC or LC but are nonetheless abnormal and clinically symptomatic. Increasingly recognised as a real entity.
distinguishing_features: Biopsy findings fall short of standard diagnostic thresholds
Symptoms indistinguishable from CC or LC
May be a transitional or early-stage form

subgroups.name: Drug-induced microscopic colitis
subgroups.description: A subset where a specific medication is the identifiable trigger and withdrawal leads to resolution of symptoms.
distinguishing_features: Temporal link between starting a drug and onset of diarrhoea
Most commonly implicated: NSAIDs, PPIs, SSRIs (particularly sertraline), statins, ACE inhibitors
Symptoms often resolve within weeks of stopping the culprit drug
Biopsy may normalise after drug withdrawal
Recurrence on re-challenge with the drug confirms causality

symptoms.notes: Typically 5–10 or more loose or watery stools per day; the absence of blood is a distinguishing feature from IBD like ulcerative colitis
Waking from sleep to defecate is a red flag symptom that strongly suggests organic rather than functional disease; helps distinguish MC from IBS
Urgency can be severe and socially disabling; incontinence is reported by a substantial proportion of those with active disease
Often diffuse; tends to accompany or precede loose stools rather than being constant
Common but non-specific; may overlap with coexisting conditions such as SIBO or coeliac disease
Often related to disrupted sleep from nocturnal symptoms, nutritional depletion, or the systemic burden of chronic inflammation
More common in severe or longstanding active disease; can result from reduced absorption and reduced food intake due to symptom fear
Reported by a subset; may be related to the underlying condition or to medications used to treat it
May reflect the broader autoimmune context; more common in those with coexisting autoimmune conditions
Risk increases with high stool volume; electrolyte disturbance is possible in severe flares

key_patterns: Chronic watery non-bloody diarrhoea persisting for weeks to months, particularly in women over 50
Normal or near-normal colonoscopy appearance despite active diarrhoea — this combination should always prompt biopsy
Onset temporally linked to starting a new medication (NSAID, PPI, SSRI, statin)
Coexisting autoimmune conditions such as coeliac disease or thyroid disease
Blood tests may be normal or show mild elevation in CRP, ESR, or low-normal albumin — absence of raised inflammatory markers does not exclude MC
Calprotectin may be mildly elevated or normal — a normal result does not rule out MC
Nocturnal diarrhoea pattern raising suspicion of organic rather than functional aetiology

at_home.name: Symptom and medication diary
at_home.description: Tracking daily stool frequency, consistency (using the Bristol Stool Scale), timing (including any nocturnal episodes), food intake, and all medications and supplements. Noting when symptoms started relative to any medication changes is particularly valuable.
at_home.safety_notes: Purely observational; no risk. The diary is most useful when brought to a clinical consultation and can accelerate the diagnostic process significantly.

at_home.name: Provisional removal of suspected drug triggers
at_home.description: If a person is taking NSAIDs (including OTC ibuprofen or aspirin), PPIs, or SSRIs and their diarrhoea started after beginning one of these, discussing with a prescribing clinician whether it is safe to trial stopping or substituting the drug. Symptom improvement within 2–4 weeks may support a drug-induced mechanism.
at_home.safety_notes: Should not be done unilaterally with prescription medications, particularly SSRIs (which require tapering) or medications managing serious conditions. Always discuss with the prescribing clinician before stopping any prescribed drug.

clinical.name: Colonoscopy with multiple segmental biopsies
clinical.description: The definitive diagnostic test. The colon typically appears macroscopically normal or near-normal, so biopsy is essential. Multiple biopsies from the right colon (ascending colon, hepatic flexure), transverse colon, and left colon are needed because the histological changes can be patchy. The pathologist assesses collagen band thickness (for CC) and intraepithelial lymphocyte counts (for LC). Sampling only the sigmoid or rectum risks missing the diagnosis.
clinical.availability: Ordered by a gastroenterologist. Widely available in secondary care. The key is to ensure the requesting clinician specifically asks for biopsies and informs the pathologist of the clinical suspicion — colonoscopy reported as 'normal' without biopsy cannot exclude MC.

clinical.name: Flexible sigmoidoscopy with biopsies
clinical.description: A less invasive alternative that examines the left colon only. May miss a proportion of MC cases because changes are more prominent in the right colon, particularly for collagenous colitis. Can be useful when full colonoscopy is not immediately available but is not the preferred first-line investigation.
clinical.availability: Available in most secondary care endoscopy units; can sometimes be arranged more quickly than full colonoscopy.

clinical.name: Faecal calprotectin
clinical.description: A stool test that measures intestinal inflammation. In MC, calprotectin may be mildly to moderately elevated or within normal range — it is neither sensitive nor specific enough to diagnose or exclude MC. Most useful in the initial workup to help distinguish organic from functional causes, and to monitor treatment response in those with confirmed MC.
clinical.availability: Widely available via GP or gastroenterology referral; some private labs offer direct-to-consumer testing.

clinical.name: Coeliac serology (tTG-IgA + total IgA)
clinical.description: Coeliac disease co-occurs with microscopic colitis at significantly higher rates than the general population. Testing for tissue transglutaminase antibodies (with total IgA to exclude IgA deficiency) is part of standard workup and should be performed before any gluten-free diet is started.
clinical.availability: Standard blood test available via GP. Must be performed while still consuming gluten to avoid false negatives.

clinical.name: Thyroid function tests (TSH, T4)
clinical.description: Thyroid disorders — both hypo- and hyperthyroidism — are more common in people with MC. Thyroid dysfunction can also independently cause or worsen diarrhoea. Routine screening is reasonable during workup.
clinical.availability: Standard blood test available via GP.

clinical.name: SeHCAT scan (selenium homocholic acid taurine)
clinical.description: Tests for bile acid malabsorption, which may coexist with MC and contribute to watery diarrhoea. A proportion of people with MC have evidence of BAM, and treating it alongside MC may improve outcomes. SeHCAT involves swallowing a radiolabelled bile acid capsule and measuring retention at day 7 with a gamma camera.
clinical.availability: Available in specialist gastroenterology centres; more widely used in the UK than the US. An empirical trial of cholestyramine or colesevelam is sometimes used instead where SeHCAT is unavailable.

clinical.name: Full blood count, CRP, ESR, albumin
clinical.description: Basic inflammatory and nutritional markers. Often normal in MC — importantly, a normal CRP does not exclude the diagnosis. Anaemia, if present, may suggest an alternative or coexisting diagnosis. These tests help rule out other causes and assess nutritional impact.
clinical.availability: Standard blood tests available via GP.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: MC is one of the most important organic conditions to exclude before accepting an IBS-D diagnosis. Both cause chronic watery diarrhoea with abdominal discomfort and normal-looking colonoscopy. MC is distinguished by nocturnal diarrhoea, larger stool volumes, and histological changes on biopsy. Many people with MC are initially misdiagnosed with IBS-D and managed symptomatically for years.

related_conditions.name: Coeliac disease
related_conditions.explanation: Coeliac disease co-occurs with microscopic colitis at significantly elevated rates — estimates suggest 5–10 times the rate in the general population. Both are autoimmune, share HLA-DQ2/DQ8 risk alleles, and can cause chronic diarrhoea. Lymphocytic colitis in particular is strongly associated with coeliac disease, and some cases of LC may resolve with a strict gluten-free diet.

related_conditions.name: Bile acid malabsorption (BAM)
related_conditions.explanation: A meaningful proportion of people with MC have coexisting BAM, which contributes to watery diarrhoea through a separate mechanism. Treating BAM with bile acid sequestrants alongside MC-specific therapy may produce better outcomes than treating either condition alone.

related_conditions.name: Thyroid dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Autoimmune thyroid diseases (Hashimoto's thyroiditis and Graves' disease) occur more frequently in people with MC than in the general population, consistent with shared autoimmune mechanisms. Thyroid dysfunction can also independently worsen diarrhoea and fatigue.

related_conditions.name: IBD (Crohn's disease / ulcerative colitis)
related_conditions.explanation: MC is classified as a form of inflammatory bowel disease but is biologically distinct from Crohn's and ulcerative colitis. It does not cause bloody diarrhoea, does not produce endoscopically visible mucosal changes, and carries a much better long-term prognosis. However, all three fall under the IBD umbrella and share some overlapping immune mechanisms.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS)
related_conditions.explanation: Increased mast cell activity has been observed in the colonic mucosa of some people with MC. MCAS may coexist and contribute to symptom burden, particularly in those with multisystem symptoms alongside their bowel disease. Some practitioners explore mast cell stabilisation as an adjunct in refractory MC.

related_conditions.name: Non-coeliac gluten sensitivity (NCGS)
related_conditions.explanation: In people with lymphocytic colitis who do not have coeliac disease, gluten may still play a triggering role through non-immune mechanisms. Some individuals with LC and NCGS report improvement on a gluten-free diet, though evidence is limited and individual responses vary.

related_conditions.name: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO)
related_conditions.explanation: SIBO can coexist with MC and produce overlapping symptoms including bloating and diarrhoea. Altered gut motility and immune function in MC may create conditions that favour bacterial overgrowth. When MC treatment does not fully resolve symptoms, SIBO should be considered as a contributing factor.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: Luminal bacteria are thought to be one of the triggers for the abnormal immune response in MC. Altered gut microbiome composition has been observed in people with MC, and dysbiosis may perpetuate inflammation even after drug triggers are removed.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Living with unpredictable, high-frequency diarrhoea and urgency is a significant psychological burden. Anxiety — particularly around bowel urgency and social situations — is commonly reported by people with MC and can worsen symptom perception and quality of life.

related_conditions.name: Fibromyalgia
related_conditions.explanation: Fibromyalgia and other widespread pain conditions have been reported at elevated rates in people with MC, potentially reflecting shared neuroimmune dysregulation or the chronic burden of an active inflammatory condition.

strategies.name: Identify and withdraw drug triggers
strategies.description: Reviewing all current medications — including OTC drugs and supplements — for known MC triggers is the most important first step. NSAIDs (aspirin, ibuprofen, naproxen), proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole, lansoprazole), SSRIs (particularly sertraline, paroxetine), statins, and ACE inhibitors are the most commonly implicated. Stopping the offending drug, when clinically safe, often produces significant improvement within weeks and may lead to full remission.
strategies.notes: Should always be done in consultation with the prescribing clinician. Some medications cannot be safely stopped or require tapering. Do not stop SSRIs abruptly.

strategies.name: Budesonide
strategies.description: An oral corticosteroid with predominantly local action in the colon. Budesonide is the most evidence-supported pharmacological treatment for MC. A typical course is 9mg daily for 6–8 weeks, followed by tapering. High remission rates are seen, but relapse after stopping is common — many people require maintenance therapy at lower doses (3–6mg).
strategies.notes: Unlike systemic corticosteroids, budesonide has lower systemic exposure due to high first-pass hepatic metabolism, but long-term use still carries risks including adrenal suppression and bone density loss. Requires prescription and monitoring.

strategies.name: Bismuth subsalicylate
strategies.description: Available OTC (as Pepto-Bismol), bismuth subsalicylate has shown benefit in small studies for MC, particularly for mild-to-moderate disease. Typical doses used in studies are higher than standard OTC recommendations. May be a useful option in people who cannot tolerate or access budesonide.
strategies.notes: Contains salicylate — avoid in people sensitive to salicylates or taking warfarin. High-dose prolonged use can carry neurotoxicity risk. Discuss dosing with a clinician.

strategies.name: Bile acid sequestrants (cholestyramine, colesevelam)
strategies.description: Useful in the subset of people with MC who also have coexisting bile acid malabsorption. By binding bile acids in the gut, these agents can reduce watery diarrhoea. An empirical trial is often warranted even without a formal SeHCAT test.
strategies.notes: Cholestyramine can be difficult to tolerate due to taste and texture. Must be taken separately from other medications (at least 1–2 hours apart) as it can impair absorption. Colesevelam is better tolerated.

strategies.name: Gluten-free diet (in coeliac or NCGS context)
strategies.description: For people with MC and confirmed coeliac disease, strict adherence to a gluten-free diet is essential and may resolve lymphocytic colitis changes. Some people with LC without confirmed coeliac disease may also experience improvement on gluten withdrawal, particularly if gluten sensitivity is suspected.
strategies.notes: Coeliac serology should be checked before adopting a gluten-free diet, as the diet will cause false-negative results if started beforehand. A FODMAP-trained dietitian can help design a nutritionally complete elimination approach.

strategies.name: Low-fat, low-fibre diet during flares
strategies.description: During active high-frequency diarrhoea, reducing insoluble fibre, high-fat foods, and large meals can reduce stool frequency and urgency. Eating small, frequent meals and avoiding foods known to stimulate motility (large amounts of caffeine, alcohol, spicy foods, sugar alcohols) can help manage symptoms while awaiting medical treatment.
strategies.notes: This is a temporary comfort measure, not a treatment for the underlying inflammation. A registered dietitian can provide personalised guidance, especially to avoid nutritional deficiency during restriction.

strategies.name: Stopping smoking
strategies.description: Smoking is an independent risk factor for MC and is associated with more severe and treatment-resistant disease. Stopping smoking is consistently associated with improved outcomes and lower relapse rates.
strategies.notes: The association is stronger for collagenous colitis than lymphocytic colitis. Smoking cessation support (pharmacological and behavioural) should be offered as part of overall management.

strategies.name: Loperamide (antidiarrhoeal)
strategies.description: An OTC antidiarrhoeal that slows gut motility and reduces stool frequency and urgency. Does not treat the inflammation but can provide meaningful short-term symptom control, particularly for managing urgency and social situations.
strategies.notes: Most effective as a symptomatic adjunct rather than sole treatment. Should not be used regularly without medical oversight, particularly if there is any risk of toxic megacolon (rare in MC but worth noting).

strategies.name: Stress management and psychological support
strategies.description: While MC is an organic inflammatory disease, psychological stress can exacerbate gut motility and symptom perception. Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT), gut-directed hypnotherapy, and mindfulness-based approaches may help manage the psychological burden and reduce symptom amplification.
strategies.notes: This is not a suggestion that MC is 'psychological' — it is an inflammatory disease. However, managing the psychological impact of a chronic, unpredictable diarrhoeal illness improves quality of life and may reduce symptom severity through gut-brain axis modulation.

things_to_avoid: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, aspirin, naproxen) — among the most common drug triggers for MC; should be avoided or substituted with paracetamol/acetaminophen where possible
Proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole, lansoprazole, pantoprazole) — strongly associated with MC; review necessity with a prescribing clinician
Caffeine in large amounts — stimulates intestinal motility and can worsen urgency and stool frequency during active disease
Alcohol — can exacerbate diarrhoea and irritate the colonic mucosa
High-dose soluble and insoluble fibre supplements during active flares — can dramatically increase stool volume and frequency
Sugar alcohols (sorbitol, mannitol, xylitol) found in sugar-free products — osmotically active and can worsen watery diarrhoea
Smoking — independent risk factor for MC and associated with more severe, treatment-resistant disease
Self-discontinuing budesonide abruptly — tapering is required to avoid adrenal suppression and to manage relapse risk
Ignoring coexisting coeliac disease — untreated coeliac disease will perpetuate intestinal inflammation and prevent MC from responding to treatment

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Many people with MC spend years being told they have IBS because their colonoscopy 'looked normal' — but a colonoscopy without biopsies cannot diagnose or exclude microscopic colitis.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Patients often trust that a 'clear' colonoscopy means the bowel is genuinely normal. Clinicians may not think to biopsy a visually normal colon, particularly in a younger patient or one seen in a busy service.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Explicitly asking for biopsies at the time of colonoscopy, or requesting a repeat colonoscopy with biopsy if a previous scope was done without tissue sampling, often leads to a diagnosis within weeks — and a diagnosis opens access to effective treatments rather than indefinite IBS management.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: A medication you may have been taking for years — including a PPI prescribed for an unrelated issue — may be directly causing your MC. Treating the colitis without removing the drug trigger will produce at best partial and temporary improvement.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People trust that medications prescribed by doctors are not causing harm. PPIs in particular are perceived as benign and are often continued on autopilot. Questioning a prescribed medication feels uncomfortable or oppositional.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Working through a structured medication review with a gastroenterologist or prescribing clinician — including OTC drugs, supplements, and long-term prescriptions — and trialling withdrawal of suspected triggers under supervision. Some people experience full remission after stopping a single drug.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Budesonide is highly effective for MC, but a substantial proportion of people relapse when they stop it. For some people, this means long-term maintenance therapy — not a one-time treatment course.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: The concept of indefinite medication use is psychologically difficult, and some clinicians frame budesonide as a short-term fix. People may stop therapy prematurely when feeling well, expecting sustained remission.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Working with a gastroenterologist to establish the lowest effective maintenance dose, while simultaneously addressing all modifiable triggers (medications, diet, smoking), so that the relapse threshold is as high as possible.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Microscopic colitis is a real, biopsy-confirmed inflammatory disease — but many of its symptoms overlap with IBS and anxiety, and the psychological burden of chronic unpredictable diarrhoea can amplify symptom perception beyond the underlying inflammation alone.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: After years of being dismissed or told their symptoms are stress-related, people with MC can be understandably resistant to any framing that involves psychological contribution — even when the underlying diagnosis is clear.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Treating the inflammation first and effectively (with budesonide or drug withdrawal), then assessing what residual symptoms remain. Those who also address the psychological dimension — particularly health anxiety and hypervigilance around urgency — often achieve better functional outcomes than those who focus on the bowel alone.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Drug-induced MC can produce histologically identical findings to idiopathic MC — but removing the culprit drug alone can result in full and durable remission without any other treatment. This is the only intervention with genuine disease-modifying potential when a drug trigger is present.
key_levers.how_to_start: Create a list of all medications (prescription, OTC, and supplements) with their start dates and map them against symptom onset. Bring this to a gastroenterologist or GP and ask explicitly which can be safely withdrawn or substituted.
key_levers.timeline: Improvement often begins within 2–4 weeks of stopping the offending drug; full histological resolution may take months.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Without a biopsy-confirmed diagnosis, treatment is speculative and access to appropriate medications is limited. A diagnosis also opens the door to ruling out coexisting conditions (coeliac disease, BAM) that require separate management.
key_levers.how_to_start: If a previous colonoscopy was performed without biopsies, or only left-sided biopsies were taken, request a new colonoscopy with segmental biopsies from the right colon, transverse colon, and left colon, and ensure the pathologist is aware of the clinical suspicion for MC.
key_levers.timeline: Biopsy results typically return within 1–2 weeks of colonoscopy.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Budesonide is the most evidence-based treatment for MC with high remission rates. Achieving remission reduces nutritional depletion, restores sleep, and dramatically improves quality of life.
key_levers.how_to_start: Discuss initiation with a gastroenterologist. The standard induction dose is 9mg daily for 6–8 weeks. Plan for a tapering strategy and discuss maintenance dosing given the high relapse rate.
key_levers.timeline: Most people see meaningful improvement within 2–4 weeks of starting budesonide.

key_levers.lever: Test for and treat coeliac disease
key_levers.why_it_matters: Untreated coeliac disease perpetuates intestinal inflammation and prevents MC from responding fully to treatment. This is one of the most actionable and overlooked coexisting conditions in MC.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request tTG-IgA and total IgA blood tests through a GP before starting any gluten-free diet. If positive, proceed to duodenal biopsy to confirm. If negative but suspicion remains, ask about NCGS and whether a gluten-free trial is warranted.
key_levers.timeline: Coeliac serology takes days; biopsy results within 1–2 weeks. Histological improvement on a gluten-free diet may take 6–12 months.

key_levers.why_it_matters: A significant minority of people with MC have BAM contributing to their watery diarrhoea through a separate osmotic mechanism. Treating BAM alongside MC-specific therapy may resolve residual symptoms that persist despite budesonide.
key_levers.how_to_start: Discuss SeHCAT testing with a gastroenterologist, or ask about an empirical trial of a bile acid sequestrant (cholestyramine or colesevelam) if SeHCAT is unavailable.
key_levers.timeline: Response to bile acid sequestrants is often seen within 1–2 weeks.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Smoking is an independent modifiable risk factor for MC associated with more severe disease and treatment resistance. Stopping smoking is one of the few lifestyle changes with clear evidence of impact on disease course.
key_levers.how_to_start: Engage with a smoking cessation programme combining pharmacological support (varenicline, NRT) with behavioural coaching. Inform the gastroenterologist so that smoking status is factored into treatment decisions.
key_levers.timeline: Benefits to disease course may take months to manifest after cessation.

common_mistakes: Accepting a diagnosis of IBS-D without confirming that colonoscopy was performed with biopsies — normal macroscopic appearance does not exclude MC
Stopping budesonide as soon as symptoms resolve, without a tapering plan or maintenance strategy, leading to early relapse
Continuing NSAIDs, PPIs, or SSRIs throughout treatment without reviewing whether they can be safely withdrawn — a medication trigger will undermine any pharmacological treatment
Focusing only on dietary restriction during flares without treating the underlying inflammation — diet can manage symptoms temporarily but does not resolve the mucosal disease
Not testing for coeliac disease before starting a gluten-free diet — the diet invalidates serology and biopsy, making later testing unreliable
Dismissing residual symptoms after MC treatment as 'still the MC' without investigating whether SIBO, BAM, or another coexisting condition is driving ongoing diarrhoea
Using high-dose soluble fibre supplements (e.g. psyllium husk) during active disease in an attempt to bulk stools — this can dramatically worsen stool volume in secretory diarrhoea

specialists.role: The specialist most likely to diagnose MC through colonoscopy and biopsy, and to initiate and manage budesonide therapy. Also coordinates investigation of coexisting conditions such as coeliac disease and BAM.
specialists.notes: The key is finding a gastroenterologist who will actually biopsy a visually normal colon. Some clinicians will reassure patients based on macroscopic appearance alone. Asking explicitly about MC and requesting biopsies before a colonoscopy takes place is often necessary. Inflammatory bowel disease specialists or those with a particular interest in chronic diarrhoea are most useful.

specialists.role: Evaluates the colonic biopsies and makes the histological diagnosis. The quality of the pathological review matters — patchy disease and incomplete MC may be missed by non-specialist pathologists.
specialists.notes: If initial biopsies are reported as normal but clinical suspicion remains high, seeking a second opinion from a specialist GI pathology unit may be worthwhile.

specialists.role: Supports dietary management during active disease and remission, including managing symptom-triggering foods, ensuring nutritional adequacy during restriction, and guiding gluten-free diet adherence in those with coexisting coeliac disease.
specialists.notes: A dietitian cannot treat the inflammation itself, but is important for quality of life and nutritional health, particularly in people with prolonged active disease or multiple dietary restrictions.

specialists.role: Conducts initial blood tests, reviews and adjusts medication triggers (in consultation with relevant specialists), makes referrals, and manages long-term monitoring of patients on budesonide.
specialists.notes: Many GPs are less familiar with MC than with IBD or IBS. Bringing printed evidence-based information (such as BSG or AGA guidelines) to consultations and specifically requesting colonoscopy with biopsy can be effective strategies.

specialists.type: Functional medicine doctor or integrative gastroenterologist
specialists.role: May explore upstream drivers more thoroughly, including gut dysbiosis, food sensitivities, and comprehensive medication and supplement review. Can be useful for people with complex multi-system presentations or those who have not responded fully to standard treatment.
specialists.notes: Quality varies widely; most useful when used alongside, not instead of, conventional gastroenterological care. Mainstream gastroenterology provides the definitive diagnosis — functional medicine may add value in addressing residual symptoms and triggers.

patient_communities.name: Microscopic Colitis Foundation
patient_communities.notes: The most comprehensive patient-facing resource dedicated specifically to MC. Includes an active patient forum, detailed information on drug triggers, dietary guidance, and treatment options. Particularly valuable for people newly diagnosed or those with treatment-resistant disease.

patient_communities.name: r/MicroscopicColitis
patient_communities.notes: A modestly sized but active community of people living with MC. Useful for peer support, practical tips, and shared experience — particularly around medication management and navigating the diagnostic process.

patient_communities.name: Crohn's and Colitis UK — Microscopic Colitis information
patient_communities.notes: Well-written, patient-friendly overview of MC from the UK's main IBD charity. Useful for people in the UK navigating NHS pathways.

patient_communities.name: Crohn's and Colitis Foundation (US) — Microscopic Colitis
patient_communities.notes: US-based patient organisation with information and support resources for people with MC and broader IBD. Includes information on clinical trials and specialist centres.

learning_resources.title: Microscopic Colitis Foundation — Treatment and Triggers Guide
learning_resources.description: Detailed, patient-written and expert-reviewed resource covering treatment options, a comprehensive list of drug triggers, and dietary approaches. Widely considered the most practical and thorough non-academic resource on MC available.

learning_resources.title: Microscopic colitis: Pathophysiology and clinical management
learning_resources.author_or_source: Miehlke S, et al. — The Lancet Gastroenterology & Hepatology
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive review of MC covering epidemiology, pathogenesis, diagnostic criteria, and current treatment evidence. A rigorous resource for those who want to understand the clinical science behind their diagnosis.

learning_resources.title: British Society of Gastroenterology — Guidelines on Microscopic Colitis
learning_resources.description: The UK clinical practice guidelines for MC, covering diagnosis, investigation, and treatment. Useful for patients who want to understand what standard care should look like and to advocate for appropriate investigation.

learning_resources.title: American Gastroenterological Association (AGA) — Clinical Practice Update on Microscopic Colitis
learning_resources.description: US clinical update covering best practices in diagnosis and management of MC. Particularly useful for understanding which biopsies are needed and how to manage drug triggers and budesonide therapy.

learning_resources.title: Crohn's and Colitis UK — Microscopic Colitis patient information
learning_resources.description: Accessible, well-written introduction to MC covering symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment in plain language. A good starting point for people newly diagnosed or trying to understand what MC is.

sections.relevance_signal: **Chronic watery diarrhoea** — 5–10 times per day — that looks like IBS-D but does not respond to diet or IBS treatments. Normal colonoscopy appearance but abnormal biopsy.
